title: Working with the part that believes love must be earned
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part that keeps score

There's a part of me that still keeps track. It's quiet most days, but it's always watching. It notices when I forget to say thank you. It counts the times I cancel plans. It tallies up every moment I fall short of being good enough, kind enough, present enough. And then it whispers: You owe them. You haven't earned your place here yet.

This part believes love is transactional. It learned this somewhere, probably early, probably before I had words for what was happening. Maybe you know this part too. It's the one that makes you say yes when you want to say no. The one that apologizes for taking up space. The one that works twice as hard just to feel like you deserve to be in the room.

Here's the thing. This part isn't wrong about what it learned. It learned that love comes with conditions because that's what it experienced. Maybe your parents only showed affection when you got good grades. Maybe your partner's warmth depended on you being agreeable. Maybe your friendships required you to be the listener, the fixer, the one who never had needs of their own.

Richard Schwartz, who developed Internal Family Systems (IFS), calls these parts "protectors." They took on a job to keep you safe. The part that believes love must be earned is trying to prevent abandonment. It's trying to ensure you're not rejected. It's doing its job. But its job description was written by a child who didn't have other options.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts. It helped me understand that even this exhausting, anxious part isn't my enemy. It's just stuck in an old strategy.

Where this part learned its math

Think about the first time you felt like love had to be earned. Can you feel it in your body? That tightening in your chest. That hollow feeling in your stomach. That's the memory living in your nervous system.

Bessel van der Kolk, the trauma researcher who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, showed us that trauma isn't just a story we remember. It's a physical imprint. The part that believes love must be earned isn't just a thought pattern. It's a survival strategy that got wired into your body's alarm system.

When you were young, you needed attachment more than anything. You needed your caregivers to stay close, to protect you, to feed you. If their love felt conditional, your brain made a logical conclusion: I need to perform to survive. This isn't a character flaw. It's a biological adaptation.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets trapped in the body. The part that believes love must be earned is holding a frozen response. It's still waiting for you to do the right thing so it can finally relax. But the conditions keep changing. The goalpost keeps moving. You can never do enough because the original situation wasn't about what you did. It was about what you needed and didn't get.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents. Reading it felt like someone finally explained the rules of a game I'd been losing my whole life.

Stay with me here. This part isn't stupid. It's incredibly smart. It figured out a way to keep you connected to people who couldn't offer unconditional love. That's a survival adaptation. But you're not that child anymore. You don't need to earn love to survive. The part just doesn't know that yet.

The exhaustion of perpetual earning

Let's be honest about what this costs. Living with a part that believes love must be earned is exhausting. It's like having a full-time job you never applied for. You're always on shift. You're always scanning for approval. You're always measuring your worth against invisible standards.

Here's what this looks like in daily life. You overexplain yourself in emails. You rehearse conversations before they happen. You apologize for things that aren't your fault. You say yes to social events you dread because saying no feels like a betrayal. You check your phone obsessively after texting someone, waiting for a response that proves you're still accepted.

I've done all of this. I still do some of it. The part doesn't just disappear when you understand it. It needs something more than insight. It needs to be felt. It needs to be seen. It needs to know that you, the adult you, are here now and you're not going to abandon it the way others abandoned you.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did something soften? Did something tighten? That's the part responding. It's listening. It wants to know if you're really going to show up for it.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook. It has exercises that helped me start talking to this part instead of fighting it.

What this part actually needs

The part that believes love must be earned doesn't need to be argued with. It doesn't need you to prove it wrong with evidence. It doesn't need a logical rebuttal about how you're worthy just because you exist. It's heard all that before. It doesn't believe you.

What it needs is your presence. It needs you to turn toward it with curiosity instead of contempt. It needs you to ask, "What are you afraid would happen if you stopped trying so hard?" And then it needs you to listen without rushing to fix it.

Janina Fisher, who wrote Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors, teaches that parts need acknowledgment before they can transform. They need to be welcomed into the room. They need to know they have a seat at the table, not that they're being evicted.

So try this. Next time you feel that familiar drive to earn love, pause. Don't act on it. Just notice it. Say something like, "I see you there. I know you're trying to keep me safe. Thank you. I'm going to handle this differently now."

Wild, right? The part might freak out at first. It might scream that you're being reckless. That's okay. Let it panic. Don't abandon it. Just stay with it. Tell it you're not going to do what it says, but you're not going to leave it alone either.

This is the work. It's not about getting rid of the part. It's about building a relationship with it where you lead, not it. You're the adult now. You get to decide what love looks like.

What happens when you stop earning

I'm not going to tell you that everything gets better when you stop trying to earn love. Some relationships will fall apart. Some people will be confused by your new boundaries. Some will accuse you of changing, of being selfish, of not caring anymore.

That's the test. That's where you find out which relationships were based on your performance and which were based on actual connection. It's painful. It's disorienting. And it's necessary.

Here's what I've found on the other side. The relationships that survive your refusal to earn love are the ones worth keeping. They're the ones where you can show up tired and cranky and still feel welcome. They're the ones where you can say no and the person doesn't punish you. They're the ones where love is given freely, not as a reward for good behavior.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman. It helped me understand that recovery isn't about becoming perfect. It's about reclaiming your right to exist without conditions.

Your part that believes love must be earned will probably never fully disappear. It might always be there, whispering its old warnings. But over time, its voice gets quieter. Its grip gets looser. You learn to hear it without obeying it. You learn to thank it for its service and then do something different anyway.

That's not a neat ending. I know. You probably wanted me to tell you exactly how to make this part go away forever. But that's not how parts work. That's not how trauma works. That's not how love works. Love isn't earned. It's given. And the first person who needs to give it to you is you.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
